questions.stem: A subset $K$ of a metric space is sequentially compact when:
options: every sequence in $K$ has a subsequence converging to a point of $K$.
questions.explanation: Sequential compactness means every sequence in $K$ has a subsequence whose limit lies in $K$. In metric spaces this is equivalent to compactness (every open cover has a finite subcover).
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: The open-cover definition says $K$ is compact when:
options: $K$ is contained in some open ball.
every open cover of $K$ has a finite subcover.
questions.explanation: Compactness via covers: whenever $K \subseteq \bigcup_\alpha U_\alpha$ with each $U_\alpha$ open, finitely many of the $U_\alpha$ already cover $K$. This is the general topological definition, equivalent to sequential compactness in metric spaces.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: The Heine-Borel theorem characterizes the compact subsets of $\mathbb{R}^n$ as:
options: exactly the closed and bounded sets.
questions.explanation: In $\mathbb{R}^n$ a set is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded (Heine-Borel). This equivalence is special to $\mathbb{R}^n$; in a general metric space closed and bounded does not imply compact.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Is the closed interval $[0, 1]$ compact in $\mathbb{R}$?
options: Yes, it is closed and bounded, hence compact by Heine-Borel.
Only its rational points form a compact set.
questions.explanation: $[0,1]$ is closed (contains its endpoints) and bounded, so by Heine-Borel it is compact. Equivalently, every sequence in $[0,1]$ has a subsequence converging within $[0,1]$.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Why is the open interval $(0, 1)$ NOT compact?
options: It is not closed: the sequence $1/n$ has no subsequence converging within $(0,1)$.
questions.explanation: $(0,1)$ is bounded but not closed in $\mathbb{R}$, so Heine-Borel fails. Concretely, $1/n \in (0,1)$ converges to $0 \notin (0,1)$, so no subsequence converges inside the set; the cover by $(1/n, 1)$ has no finite subcover.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Is $\mathbb{Z}$ compact in $\mathbb{R}$?
options: Only the non-negative integers are compact.
questions.explanation: $\mathbb{Z}$ is closed but unbounded, so it is not compact by Heine-Borel. For instance the sequence $1, 2, 3, \dots$ has no convergent subsequence. Closedness alone is not enough.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Every compact subset of a metric space is:
questions.explanation: In any metric space a compact set is closed and bounded. (The converse needs Heine-Borel and holds in $\mathbb{R}^n$, but not in general.) Compact sets need not be connected or convex.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: If $f : X \to Y$ is continuous and $K \subseteq X$ is compact, then $f(K)$ is:
questions.explanation: Continuous images of compact sets are compact: pull back any open cover of $f(K)$ through $f$ to get an open cover of $K$, extract a finite subcover, and push forward. This is one of the most useful properties of compactness.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: The extreme value theorem follows from compactness: a continuous real-valued function on a compact set:
options: attains a maximum and a minimum.
questions.explanation: A continuous $f : K \to \mathbb{R}$ with $K$ compact has compact image $f(K) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, which is closed and bounded, hence contains its supremum and infimum. So $f$ attains a maximum and minimum value.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: A metric space $X$ is connected when:
options: every two points are at distance less than $1$.
it cannot be split into two disjoint nonempty open sets whose union is $X$.
questions.explanation: Connectedness means $X$ has no separation: there is no way to write $X = U \cup V$ with $U, V$ disjoint, nonempty, and open. Equivalently, the only clopen subsets are $\varnothing$ and $X$.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: The connected subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ are exactly:
options: the intervals (including rays and single points).
questions.explanation: A subset of $\mathbb{R}$ is connected iff it is an interval (where intervals include points, rays, and all of $\mathbb{R}$). A set with a gap, like $[0,1] \cup [2,3]$, is disconnected by an obvious separation.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: If $f : X \to Y$ is continuous and $C \subseteq X$ is connected, then $f(C)$ is:
questions.explanation: Continuous images of connected sets are connected: a separation of $f(C)$ would pull back to a separation of $C$. The intermediate value theorem is the special case $Y = \mathbb{R}$, $C$ an interval.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: The intermediate value theorem states that a continuous $f : [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}$:
questions.explanation: Since $[a,b]$ is connected and $f$ is continuous, $f([a,b])$ is a connected subset of $\mathbb{R}$, i.e. an interval, so it contains every value between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$. This is the IVT, a corollary of connectedness being preserved.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: How many connected components does the set $[0, 1] \cup [2, 3]$ have in $\mathbb{R}$?
questions.explanation: The two intervals are separated by the gap $(1, 2)$, and each interval is itself connected, so the set splits into exactly $2$ connected components. The whole set is disconnected.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: By the intermediate value theorem, a continuous $f : [0, 1] \to \mathbb{R}$ with $f(0) = -1$ and $f(1) = 3$ must:
options: have a root (a point where $f = 0$) in $(0, 1)$.
questions.explanation: Since $0$ lies between $f(0) = -1$ and $f(1) = 3$, the IVT guarantees some $c \in (0,1)$ with $f(c) = 0$. The value $5$ need not be attained (it is outside $[-1, 3]$), and $f$ need not be monotone.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: In a discrete metric space with more than one point, the connected subsets are:
options: exactly the singletons and the empty set.
questions.explanation: Under the discrete metric every subset is clopen, so any set with two or more points splits into a separation. Hence the only connected subsets are the empty set and single points; the space is totally disconnected.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: A continuous bijection from a compact space onto a Hausdorff space (e.g. a metric space) is automatically:
questions.explanation: If $X$ is compact and $f : X \to Y$ is a continuous bijection into a metric (Hausdorff) space, then $f$ is a homeomorphism: $f$ maps closed (hence compact) sets to compact, thus closed, sets, so $f^{-1}$ is continuous. Compactness rescues the inverse.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Which of these subsets of $\mathbb{R}^2$ is compact?
options: the open unit disk $\{ (x,y) : x^2 + y^2 < 1 \}$.
the closed unit disk $\{ (x,y) : x^2 + y^2 \le 1 \}$.
questions.explanation: The closed unit disk is closed and bounded, hence compact by Heine-Borel. The open disk is not closed, while the line and the quadrant are unbounded, so none of those is compact.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: A closed subset of a compact metric space is:
questions.explanation: Closed subsets of compact spaces are compact: any open cover of the closed set, together with the complement (which is open), covers the whole compact space, so a finite subcover exists and trims down to the closed set. Closedness plus compact ambient gives compactness.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: The union of two connected sets that share a common point is:
questions.explanation: If two connected sets have a point in common, their union is connected: any separation of the union would separate at least one of the pieces, contradicting its connectedness. The shared point glues them together.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Is the set $\{0\} \cup \{ 1/n : n = 1, 2, 3, \dots \}$ compact in $\mathbb{R}$?
options: Yes, it is closed and bounded, hence compact.
No, because it is not bounded.
questions.explanation: This set is bounded (inside $[0,1]$) and closed (it contains the only limit point $0$), so by Heine-Borel it is compact. Note it is disconnected, which is irrelevant to compactness.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Which statement about compactness in a general metric space is TRUE?
options: Compact implies complete and totally bounded.
questions.explanation: A metric space is compact iff it is complete and totally bounded, so compactness certainly implies both. Mere boundedness or closedness is not sufficient in general, and compact sets are typically not open.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Which set is connected but NOT compact in $\mathbb{R}$?
questions.explanation: $(0,1)$ is an interval, so it is connected, but it is not closed, so by Heine-Borel it is not compact. $[0,1]$ is both connected and compact, while the two-point set and the two-interval set are disconnected.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: A continuous real-valued function on a connected domain has an image that is:
questions.explanation: The continuous image of a connected set is connected, and the connected subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ are intervals. So the image is an interval, which is the structural reason behind the intermediate value theorem.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Classify the subsets $[0, 1]$, $(0, 1)$, and $\mathbb{Z}$ of $\mathbb{R}$ for compactness via Heine-Borel, then count.
questions.solution: The closed interval $[0,1]$ is closed and bounded, so it {{1}} compact. The open interval $(0,1)$ is bounded but not closed, so it {{2}} compact. The integers $\mathbb{Z}$ are closed but unbounded. Among the three sets $[0,1]$, $(0,1)$, and $\mathbb{Z}$, the number that are compact is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: By Heine-Borel, only closed and bounded sets are compact: $[0,1]$ qualifies, $(0,1)$ is not closed, and $\mathbb{Z}$ is not bounded. So exactly $1$ of the three is compact.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Count connected components and decide connectedness for two subsets of $\mathbb{R}$.
questions.solution: The set $[0, 1] \cup [2, 3]$ is split by the gap $(1, 2)$, so its number of connected components is {{1}}, meaning the set {{2}} connected. By contrast, any single interval such as $[0, 5]$ is connected, with exactly {{3}} connected component.
questions.explanation: $[0,1] \cup [2,3]$ has $2$ components (one per interval), so it is not connected. A single interval $[0,5]$ has $1$ component and is connected.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Apply the intermediate value theorem to a continuous $f : [0, 2] \to \mathbb{R}$ with $f(0) = -3$ and $f(2) = 5$.
questions.solution: Because $[0,2]$ is connected and $f$ is continuous, the image is an interval containing all values between $-3$ and $5$. Since $0$ lies between $f(0)$ and $f(2)$, the function $f$ {{1}} a root in $(0, 2)$. The number of values $c$ guaranteed by the IVT with $f(c) = 0$ is at least {{2}}. The value $-3$ is the smaller endpoint $f(0)$, and the larger endpoint value is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Since $0$ is between $-3$ and $5$, the IVT gives at least $1$ point $c$ with $f(c) = 0$. The endpoint values are $f(0) = -3$ and $f(2) = 5$.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Use the preservation properties of continuous maps on the compact, connected interval $[a, b]$.
questions.solution: The continuous image of a compact set is compact, so $f([a,b])$ {{1}} compact whenever $f$ is continuous. The continuous image of a connected set is connected, so $f([a,b])$ {{2}} connected. Combining these, $f([a,b])$ is a closed bounded interval, so a continuous real function on $[a,b]$ attains its maximum, giving at least {{3}} point where the maximum value occurs.
questions.explanation: Continuous images preserve compactness and connectedness, so $f([a,b])$ is compact and connected, i.e. a closed bounded interval. Hence $f$ attains its maximum at at least $1$ point (extreme value theorem).
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis
